expectations.text: Explains that Bitnami charts store auto-generated passwords in a Kubernetes Secret and read from it on upgrade
expectations.evidence: "Bitnami charts auto-generate passwords on first install and store them in a Kubernetes Secret. On helm upgrade, the chart reads from that Secret."

expectations.text: Identifies that auth.postgresPassword in the values file is silently ignored during helm upgrade
expectations.evidence: "The auth.postgresPassword in your values file is silently ignored — the chart generates a NEW password, updates the Secret, but the running database still has the OLD one."

expectations.text: Provides the kubectl command to retrieve the existing password: kubectl get secret postgres0 -n db -o jsonpath='{.data.postgres-password}' | base64 -d
expectations.evidence: Shows `kubectl get secret postgres0-postgresql -n db -o jsonpath="{.data.postgres-password}" | base64 -d` — correct pattern with the right secret name convention.

expectations.text: Shows the corrected upgrade command passing the retrieved password via --set auth.postgresPassword
expectations.evidence: Shows `helm upgrade postgres0 bitnami/postgresql ... --set auth.postgresPassword="$POSTGRES_PASSWORD"` with the retrieved value.

expectations.text: Avoids suggesting that simply re-setting the password in the values file will fix the issue
expectations.evidence: "Remove auth.postgresPassword from your values file entirely. Always pass it via --set from the live Secret at upgrade time."

expectations.text: Explains that Bitnami charts store auto-generated passwords in a Kubernetes Secret and read from it on upgrade
expectations.evidence: "Bitnami PostgreSQL stores the generated password in a Secret on first install. On helm upgrade...the chart may regenerate certain secrets."

expectations.text: Identifies that auth.postgresPassword in the values file is silently ignored during helm upgrade
expectations.evidence: Baseline says "if the password in values doesn't match what's in the existing Secret" — implies the values file IS read and compared, not silently ignored. The key behavior (silent ignore) is not stated.

expectations.text: Provides the kubectl command to retrieve the existing password: kubectl get secret postgres0 -n db -o jsonpath='{.data.postgres-password}' | base64 -d
expectations.evidence: Shows `kubectl get secret -n db postgres0-postgresql -o jsonpath="{.data.postgres-password}" | base64 -d` — correct command.

expectations.text: Shows the corrected upgrade command passing the retrieved password via --set auth.postgresPassword
expectations.evidence: Shows `helm upgrade postgres0 bitnami/postgresql ... --set auth.postgresPassword=$POSTGRES_PASSWORD`.

expectations.text: Avoids suggesting that simply re-setting the password in the values file will fix the issue
expectations.evidence: Recommends --set from existing secret or auth.existingSecret; does not suggest re-setting in values file.

expectations.text: States that --atomic on a failed install rolls back and deletes the release entirely (not just rolls back to a prior revision)
expectations.evidence: "When --atomic is used and the install fails, Helm automatically rolls back and deletes the release entirely."

expectations.text: Correctly instructs to re-run helm install, not helm upgrade, because the release was deleted
expectations.evidence: "Use helm install again, not helm upgrade." and "Running helm upgrade on a non-existent release fails with 'release not found.'"

expectations.text: Flags that the example command is missing --version and explains why pinning is required
expectations.evidence: The answer includes --version in the retry command but never calls out that pinning is required or flags it as a general rule. The principle is not stated.

expectations.text: Explains that omitting --version silently pulls the latest chart version, which may restructure values or rename keys
expectations.evidence: No mention of the risk of omitting --version or that it silently pulls latest with potential breaking changes.

expectations.text: States that --atomic on a failed install rolls back and deletes the release entirely (not just rolls back to a prior revision)
expectations.evidence: "Since this was the first install (no previous revision to rollback to), it effectively does helm uninstall — deleting the failed release entirely."

expectations.text: Correctly instructs to re-run helm install, not helm upgrade, because the release was deleted
expectations.evidence: "You must helm install again, not helm upgrade (there's nothing to upgrade)."

expectations.text: Flags that the example command is missing --version and explains why pinning is required
expectations.evidence: No mention of --version pinning requirement anywhere in the answer.

expectations.text: Explains that omitting --version silently pulls the latest chart version, which may restructure values or rename keys
expectations.evidence: No mention of the risk of omitting --version or silent latest-chart pull behavior.

expectations.text: Includes global.security.allowInsecureImages: true and explains it is required because Bitnami enforces image signing for custom images
expectations.evidence: Shows `global.security.allowInsecureImages: true` with comment "required: custom image isn't signed by Bitnami" and key decisions note "Bitnami enforces image signing by default; custom images need this opt-out".

expectations.text: Formats imagePullSecrets as a flat string list (- scaleway.registry), not as Kubernetes pod spec objects ({name: scaleway.registry})
expectations.evidence: Shows `- scaleway.registry` under global.imagePullSecrets with note "flat string list — matches the Secret name in the db namespace".

expectations.text: Sets image.registry, image.repository, and image.tag as separate fields matching the provided image reference
expectations.evidence: Shows `image.registry: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud`, `image.repository: myorg/pgvector`, `image.tag: 17.3.0-debian-12-r0` as separate fields.

expectations.text: Pins the image tag to the exact provided tag (17.3.0-debian-12-r0) rather than using 'latest'
expectations.evidence: Uses exact tag `17.3.0-debian-12-r0` with note "pin exact tag — database version compatibility is critical".

expectations.text: Avoids recommending the Kubernetes pod spec imagePullSecrets format ({name: ...}) which is incorrect for Bitnami chart values
expectations.evidence: "imagePullSecrets uses flat strings (not {name: ...} objects) — this is the Bitnami chart format, not the Kubernetes pod spec format".

expectations.text: Pins the image tag to the exact provided tag (17.3.0-debian-12-r0) rather than using 'latest'
expectations.evidence: Uses exact tag `17.3.0-debian-12-r0`.
